children.name: Gavia
children.description: Divers (loons) — ancient, supremely specialized diving birds that are among the most aquatically adapted of all birds. Divers' feet are set so far back on the body that they are barely able to stand upright on land; they come ashore only to nest at the very edge of the water. Their bones are unusually solid compared to most birds, reducing buoyancy and aiding diving — divers can reach depths of 70 metres and hold their breath for several minutes. Sweden holds internationally significant breeding populations of two diver species, the red-throated and black-throated diver, both of which require clean, fish-rich lakes in boreal and sub-Arctic zones free from human disturbance. Their eerie wailing and yodelling calls are among the most primordially atmospheric sounds in Swedish nature. The great northern diver — North America's iconic 'loon' — winters off the Swedish coast in small but regular numbers.

children.name: Gavia stellata
children.description: The smålom — Sweden's smaller and more widespread breeding diver. Unique among divers in nesting on tiny bog pools that lack fish entirely, flying to larger lakes and the coast to feed. Its mournful, wailing calls — often given in flight — are the quintessential sound of the Lapland summer. In breeding plumage the plain grey head, white-streaked neck and brick-red throat patch are distinctive. Winters on the Swedish coast, particularly the Kattegat and Öresund, where thousands can concentrate in good feeding areas.

children.name: Gavia arctica
children.description: The storlom — Sweden's most elegant breeding diver, with its beautifully chequered black-and-white back and iridescent black throat patch among the most striking breeding plumages of any European bird. Requires larger, cleaner, more oligotrophic lakes than the red-throated diver and breeds throughout the Swedish forest zone. Its haunting, haunting yodelling wail — one of the most spine-tingling sounds in Swedish nature — echoes across remote northern lakes on summer evenings. Sweden holds one of Europe's most important breeding populations, making habitat protection critical. Winters on the coast.

children.name: Gavia immer
children.description: The islom — the iconic 'common loon' of North American lakes and Canada's national bird symbol, wintering in small but regular numbers off the Swedish coast, particularly in the Kattegat and the western Baltic. Much larger than Sweden's breeding divers, with a heavier, dagger-like bill. In winter plumage it is dark grey above and white below; its spectacular black-and-white breeding plumage is rarely seen in Swedish waters. The tremolo wail of the great northern diver — the archetypal sound of the Canadian wilderness — is occasionally heard from Swedish coastal waters in autumn as birds pass through.

children.name: Gavia pacifica
children.description: The Pacific loon — a sleek, medium-sized diver that breeds on Arctic tundra lakes across northern Alaska, Canada and northeastern Siberia, wintering along both the Pacific and Atlantic coasts. Long treated as a subspecies of the black-throated diver, it was recognised as a full species in the 1980s on the basis of its distinctive white flank patches, subtly different head pattern and allopatric breeding range. It is one of the most abundant and gregarious loons in its range, sometimes migrating in flocks of thousands along the Pacific coast. Its haunting wailing calls are a signature sound of the Arctic summer.

children.name: Gavia adamsii
children.description: The largest of all living divers, the yellow-billed loon breeds on Arctic tundra lakes from northern Alaska across Canada to Siberia and winters along ice-free coasts of the North Pacific and, rarely, northwestern Europe. Its massive, pale ivory-yellow bill — held characteristically tilted upward — immediately distinguishes it from all other loons. Named after the British surgeon and naturalist Edward Adams, who collected specimens during the 1850–1855 Franklin search expeditions in the Canadian Arctic. A scarce but regular winter visitor to Norwegian and occasionally Swedish Arctic coasts; any sighting in Swedish waters is a notable event.
